title: No-Go Theorem for Ancilla-Assisted Gaussian Enhancement in Passive-Unitary Estimation
abstract: We study the maximum quantum Fisher information (QFI) for estimating a single parameter embedded in a generic multimode lossless passive Gaussian unitary using general Gaussian probes under a signal-energy constraint. Unlike previous work, which imposed a total energy constraint on the full probe, we constrain only the transmitted signal modes while allowing an arbitrary number of locally retained ancilla modes with arbitrarily large energy. We prove that this additional freedom does not increase the maximum achievable QFI; the optimum remains identical to that attainable without extra ancilla energy. The same conclusion also extends to the sequential setting under a total energy constraint. We also characterize the family of optimal probe states and show that entanglement is not necessary to attain the optimum in the lossless setting. This extends the result of Matsubara et al. to the physically motivated signal-energy-constrained scenario and establishes a no-go theorem for ancilla-assisted Gaussian enhancement in noiseless passive-unitary estimation.
summary: This paper derives a no-go theorem for quantum parameter estimation in lossless passive-unitary channels. It proves that constraining the energy only in the signal modes, while allowing infinite energy in ancilla modes, does not improve the ultimate estimation precision (QFI). The result shows that the optimal measurement is entirely determined by the energy budget allocated to the signal modes.
main_problem: The paper investigates the maximum Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) achievable for estimating a parameter encoded in a passive-unitary transformation applied to a multimode Gaussian state.
main_result: It establishes a no-go theorem proving that allowing arbitrarily large energy in locally retained ancilla modes does not enhance the maximum QFI beyond the limit set by the signal-energy constraint.
method: The authors calculate the QFI by deriving and bounding contributions from the covariance matrix and displacement vector, utilizing matrix inequalities and analyzing the structure of optimal probe states.
model_or_system: The system models a bipartite sensing setup involving $m_A$ ancilla modes and $m_B$ signal modes, where the probe state is a pure Gaussian state evolving under a passive Gaussian unitary. The parameter is encoded via a passive Gaussian unitary acting only on the signal modes.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), $	ext{F}_\varphi$; covariance matrix ($\Gamma$); mean photon number ($ ar{n}$).
important_parameters: Mean photon number constraint ($ ar{n}$) on the signal modes; the structure of the passive Gaussian unitary transformation.
main_assumptions: The estimation process is restricted to lossless passive-unitary transformations, and the probe state is a pure Gaussian state.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the general setup involving ancilla and signal modes for the estimation process.
paper_structure: The paper analyzes the QFI by decomposing it into parts related to the covariance matrix and displacement vector, deriving upper bounds using matrix inequalities, and ultimately proving the no-go theorem by showing the optimal state requires zero signal displacement energy.
why_interesting: This work provides fundamental limits on quantum estimation precision in passive quantum channels, which is crucial for understanding the ultimate performance of quantum sensors and quantum communication protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Setup for ancilla-assisted single-parameter estimation using Gaussian probes. The probe consists of mA ancilla modes and mB signal modes. The initial state |ψin⟩is taken to be a tensor product of single-mode squeezed coherent states. By applying a passive linear interferometer R, we obtain the general pure Gaussian probe state |ψ⟩. The mB signal modes are transmitted through the passive lossless Gaussian unitary Rφ = I(A) ⊗R(B) φ , which encodes the unknown parameter φ, while the mA ancilla modes remain unchanged. The final output state is denoted by |ψφ⟩.
